He had the metadata: the frequency, duration, and timing of communications between Parren's personal correspondence address and the Substrate Assessment Unit's administrative contact system. The contact system was not the unit's head's direct account — it was the unit's general administrative address, the one that received scheduling requests and data access inquiries from authorized Archive personnel.

The contact had begun six weeks after the acknowledgment motion passed. It had continued since then at a rate of approximately once a week. The duration of each contact was short — five to ten minutes, communication length consistent with messaging rather than extended correspondence.

"She has personal voting authority over matters outside her suspended areas," Corin said. "She can contact administrative units."

"Yes," Cross said. "The contact is not prohibited. The administrative contact system is accessible to all Council members. What is notable is the timing — beginning six weeks after the acknowledgment motion, continuing weekly — and the target: the Substrate Assessment Unit, which is the unit currently under investigation for the Exchange relationship and the data discrepancy."

"She knows about the investigation."

"The investigation is in the institutional record. All Council members have access to the investigation file summary. She knows the unit is under review." He put the metadata printout on the desk. "Derel's contact with Parren is a separate question. Derel told me she hadn't spoken to Parren. I didn't ask her about the administrative contact system — I asked about direct communication. The administrative contact system is not direct communication. It's a managed queue."

"So we don't know if Derel is receiving Parren's correspondence or if it's going through a queue handler."

"We don't know the content. We know the contact is going to the unit's administrative address and we know the timing." Cross leaned back in his chair. "I'm giving this to you because Hollan should know about it and I don't have the authority to take it to Hollan directly."

"You could go through the security chain."

"I could. I'm choosing to come to you first because you're the institutional intersection point for this situation. If I go to Hollan directly, I create a parallel investigative track. If I come to you, the track is coordinated."

Corin looked at the metadata. Once a week, starting six weeks after the acknowledgment motion. Parren had been on the Council with a restricted authority profile for six months. She had used her remaining authority to file the protocol review — a move that had failed but that had correctly identified a gap. She had voted against the retroactive authorization. She had cast an abstention on the acknowledgment motion that fell outside her suspended area, which was not what he would have expected — he had expected a vote against.

The abstention. He had noted it at the time and not fully interpreted it. She had the voting authority on the motion and had chosen not to use it.

"The Exchange subsidiary structure motion," he said. "The vote is next week."

"Yes. The motion includes the built-in archive access order for the Exchange's eastern transitional zone data. If the motion passes, we have simultaneous dissolution of the subsidiary relationship and access to the Exchange's historical archive."

"Parren can vote on that motion."

"Her voting authority covers the operational contract matter — the Exchange relationship is an external affairs operational structure, not within her suspended credential areas." Cross was already ahead of this. "She will likely vote against. She'll have the votes to lose. The motion has the same support structure as the retroactive authorization."

"Unless she's been working the administrative contact channel to change something in the unit before the motion passes."

Cross looked at the metadata. "That is one interpretation. Another is that she's been monitoring the investigation's progress through the unit's administrative channel, which is slower and less complete than what she'd get through official council briefings but more direct."

"Why would she prefer a slower channel?"

"Because the administrative channel is not flagged in the investigation file. Official council briefings on the investigation are logged. Her review of official investigation briefings is visible in the council record." He paused. "The administrative contact is not in the investigation file. It's in the unit's contact log, which I found because I was reviewing the unit's full operational record for the Exchange relationship analysis."

"Which means she may not know you found it."

"It's possible she didn't account for the breadth of the review." He looked at Corin steadily. "I want to be careful about what I'm asserting. The metadata shows contact. It does not show the content or the purpose. I am not asserting misconduct. I am identifying a pattern that is notable in context."

"Hollan needs to see this."

"Yes."

"I'll take it to him today."

Cross stood. "One more thing. The unit's data processing for the fixed-array monitoring. The strengthening signal from the unrestored territories — Lian's monthly reports, the harmonic data from the coordinate. That data flows through the Substrate Assessment Unit's routing system before it reaches the committee."

"I know."

"The harmonic data from eleven days ago — the pre-extraction baseline match at the coordinate — that data was in the routing system four days ago when I ran the access review."

Corin looked at him.

"It reached the committee?"

"It reached the committee record, yes. But I'm asking whether it was flagged. The standard routing protocol includes an anomaly flag for any monitoring data that falls outside established variation parameters. The harmonic match at the coordinate is significantly outside established variation parameters." He paused. "The anomaly flag process routes significant deviations to a secondary review before they reach the committee. The secondary review should have elevated this finding."

"Lian's monthly report would have included it."

"Lian's monthly report is filed directly to the committee record. The fixed-array data goes through the unit's routing system. They're parallel paths. The monthly report arrived and was noted. The fixed-array data routing — I need to check whether the anomaly flag was applied." He looked at Corin. "If someone in the unit is choosing which flags to apply, the committee is seeing Lian's analysis but may not be seeing the full substrate data picture that the fixed array is producing."

Corin sat very still.

"Give me the access to check the flag log," Cross said. "Today, if possible."

"Go to Hollan directly on that one. I'll call him ahead of you. Tell him you're coming."

---

At the end of the day, Iris was still at her desk. The terminal showed the registry's current state — the two hundred and sixteen entries with their acknowledgment notations, the growing authorization chain documentation, the supplementary acknowledgment brief that Elara Venn's office had confirmed was in process.

Corin came in and sat down. He had been to Hollan's office twice that afternoon — once with Cross's metadata, once to authorize Cross's access to the unit's anomaly flag log. He had sent a message to the committee with a status update on the investigation's expansion. He had sent a message to Lian flagging the flag-log question so she could cross-reference with her monthly report.

He had not sent anything to Parren. There was nothing to send. The metadata was not a finding of misconduct. It was a pattern that Hollan was now tracking.

He looked at the terminal.

"Parren has six months left," he said.

"Yes."

"Cross found contact between her personal address and the unit's administrative system. Starting six weeks after the acknowledgment motion."

Iris looked at her terminal. She had a second window open — the Substrate Assessment Unit's organizational file, which she had been reviewing as part of her authorization chain documentation. "Parren was on the Council when the external affairs division was the Exchange's founding client. That's forty-four years ago — she wasn't on the Council forty-four years ago. But the external affairs division's history is her institutional territory. She's been on external affairs oversight for most of her Council service."

"She knows the unit."

"She may know people in the unit. People who have been in the unit for a long time." Iris turned to the second window. "The unit has had seven full-time staff for the past three years. Of the seven, three have been in the unit for more than fifteen years. Haren, who applied the classification nine years ago, has been there eleven years." She paused. "I'm not saying anyone in the unit is in contact with Parren. I'm saying the unit's long-tenured staff predate Derel's appointment and predate the current investigation, and some of them may have relationships with Council members from the external affairs oversight period."

"The unit has been processing the field monitoring data from the unrestored territories for seven years," Corin said. He had said this before — he was saying it again because it was the fact that held the other facts together. "If the entity's signal is strengthening in those territories — if the coordinate reading is detectable on the fixed arrays now — the unit is seeing that data. They've been seeing it since before we filed the expedition proposal."

"Yes."

"Someone in that unit decided not to flag it."

She looked at the registry on the terminal. The count. The running entry log. The acknowledgment notations.

"The Exchange subsidiary structure motion votes next week," she said. "If it passes, the archive access order is simultaneous with the dissolution. Whatever the Exchange has in its data from the eastern transitional zone, we'll have access."

"And if the unit's anomaly flag log shows selective flagging —"

"Then someone in the unit has been deciding which signals reach the committee." She looked at him. "The committee has been receiving Lian's monthly reports. The entity's signal strengthening has been in those reports. But the fixed-array data — the continuous data stream that the unit processes — if the anomaly flags have been selectively applied, the committee has been seeing the analysis but not the full substrate picture."

"They've been seeing what the unit decided to let through."

"That's the question Cross is answering."

He looked at the coordination data on her desk. Lian's harmonic analysis. The overlay panel he had been standing in front of three days ago, the eighty-year-old recording and the current signal, the pre-extraction baseline returning at a specific coordinate in the eastern communities.

"The acknowledgment is not the end," Iris said.

"I know."

"What comes next?"

She said it not as a question she didn't know the answer to but as the question that needed to be named — the question that held all the other questions, the one that was larger than the metadata and the flag log and the six months Parren had left and the Exchange archive access and the harmonic developing toward baseline over the next six months.

What came next was the Exchange archive. What came next was the flag log. What came next was whoever in the unit had been deciding what the committee saw and hadn't seen. What came next was the six months until the coordinate's harmonic reached the founding-era baseline, and the question of what happened when it did.

What came next was fifty-six billion people still in the dark.

"The motion votes next week," he said. "Cross's flag log analysis by end of week. Lian's next monthly report in three weeks. The third field expedition — if the harmonic is developing at the projected rate, a third expedition in four months." He was listing things not because she didn't know them but because saying them made them into work rather than weight. "Elara Venn's supplementary acknowledgment process, timeline unknown. Maret's notification to Drev — pending his response through the network."

"Drev said yes," Iris said. "Maret told me this afternoon. He wants to know the letters are in the record. He said yes."

Corin was quiet.

"When?"

"Maret is arranging it. She said whenever you can come to the northern district. He's not traveling easily at his age."

"Tell her I'll come. Next week, after the motion vote."

"I'll tell her."

He sat for a moment. The terminal showed the registry. The two hundred and sixteen entries, the running authorization chain, the acknowledgment notation in every field, the supplementary entry for the three letters — the letters that a man in his eighties had voted four to three to send, thirty-one years ago, and had not known were in any record until now.

"The unit is seeing the data," he said again. "The coordinate is doing something that is accelerating. If someone in the unit is deciding not to flag it, it means someone has been watching the entity heal the coordinate and choosing to keep that information inside the unit."

"Yes."

"Why?"

She looked at the Substrate Assessment Unit data.

"The founding client relationship has been operative for forty-four years," she said. "The Exchange has been receiving substrate monitoring data. The Exchange processes commercial timeline suppression contracts. If the entity's healing behavior in the unrestored territories — if the signal strengthening, the harmonic development — if that is visible in the substrate data and the Exchange has been receiving substrate data and someone in the unit has been deciding which anomalies to flag —"

She stopped.

He looked at her.

"The Exchange has been in the data-sharing relationship with the unit for forty-four years," she said. "The Exchange processes commercial suppression contracts in territories where the substrate is in a specific state. If the substrate state in the unrestored territories is changing — if the entity's healing is producing detectable changes in the substrate that the fixed-array data captures — the Exchange's commercial operations in those territories are being affected by changes that the Exchange's substrate data would show."

"And the Exchange has been receiving the substrate data."

"If someone in the unit has been selectively flagging — if they've been suppressing the anomaly flags on the entity's signal developments — the Exchange would have the raw substrate data but not the Archive committee's interpretation of it."

"Or," Corin said, "someone in the unit has been providing the Exchange with the full data, including the anomaly analysis, and the selective flagging is about keeping that full picture from the committee."

Iris looked at the terminal.

"I don't know which it is," she said. "That's what the flag log will show. And the Exchange archive access."

He stood.

"Both things need to be in the record," he said.

"Yes."

"All of it. Whatever the flag log shows. Whatever the Exchange archive holds."

"I know."

He looked at her.

"Forty-three years of your work," he said. "Twenty-three years before the registry had institutional standing. And now."

"And now," she said. Not dismissing the observation. Noting it. The record had standing it hadn't had. The entity was doing something at the coordinate. The harmonic was developing. The unit was being investigated. The Exchange archive was four days from accessible. The committee was receiving data from a routing system someone had been filtering.

And fifty-six billion people were in the dark, and the entity's signal in the unrestored territories was moving toward the rate it had moved at when it first began to heal, eighty years ago, before the extraction had started.

Both categories.

The acknowledgment in the record. The investigation ongoing. The restoration continuing at the rate it continued — slow, invisible in a day, visible in months.

"Good night," he said.

"Good night," she said.

He walked out into the corridor. The ventilation system. The tile. The Citadel moving into its night configuration, the monitoring wing's equipment cycling, the building holding itself around the work that did not stop when the day ended.

He walked home.

The questions were still open. They would be open tomorrow. The flag log would have data by the end of the week. The motion would vote next week. The harmonic would continue developing whether anyone was watching it or not.

The record would hold what it held.

And somewhere in the eastern communities' territory, in the borderlands near the coordinate that Tael had been visiting since she was twelve years old and that the entity had been doing something specific at for longer than anyone had known, the substrate frequency was moving — progressively, measurably, at the rate of a process that had been underway for twelve years and was now six months from something.

No one yet knew what the something was.

That was the next thing.
